common_causes: Fuel system running rich or lean (check fuel trims)
diagnostic_steps: 1. Scan for additional codes (P0171, P0174, P030x, P0130-P0141)
2. Check fuel trims (should be ±10%). If >±15%, diagnose fuel system first
3. Inspect exhaust system for leaks (visual inspection + listen for hissing)
4. Check upstream O2 sensor waveform (should switch 0.1-0.9V rapidly)
5. Check downstream O2 sensor waveform (should be steady ~0.45V)
6. If sensors OK and no leaks: catalyst efficiency is below threshold
7. Consider cat replacement (OEM preferred, aftermarket may re-trigger code)
codes.notes: Very common on high-mileage vehicles (>120k miles). Subaru cats often last 150k+ miles. Aftermarket cats may trigger false positives due to different heating characteristics.

codes.description: Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 or Single Sensor)
diagnostic_steps: 1. CRITICAL: Check if timing belt is intact and properly tensioned
2. Inspect cam sensor connector for corrosion or damage
3. Check sensor resistance (typically 500-2000 ohms, verify with FSM)
4. Inspect wiring harness for damage (rodent chewing common)
5. Check sensor airgap (should be 0.5-1.5mm from reluctor wheel)
6. If sensor tests OK: check timing belt alignment marks
7. Replace sensor if resistance out of spec or erratic signal
codes.notes: Subaru EJ engines: This code + rough running often indicates timing belt jumped. DO NOT drive if experiencing severe misfire - valve/piston contact possible on interference engines (EJ25D, EJ205, EJ255, EJ257). EJ22 is non-interference but still check immediately.

common_causes: Vacuum leak (intake boot, EVAP system, brake booster)
Fuel pressure low (weak pump, clogged filter)
Intake air leak after MAF sensor
diagnostic_steps: 1. Check fuel trims (STFT and LTFT). If >+20%, system is adding fuel to compensate
2. Inspect for vacuum leaks (smoke test or propane method)
3. Check intake boots for cracks (common on Subaru)
4. Clean MAF sensor with MAF-safe cleaner
5. Check fuel pressure (should be ~40 psi at idle, varies by model)
6. Inspect PCV valve and hoses
7. Check O2 sensor waveform (should switch, not stuck lean)
codes.notes: Subaru-specific: Check intake manifold gaskets (common leak point on EJ25). Also check turbo inlet hose on WRX/STI (often develops cracks).

diagnostic_steps: 1. Check for cylinder-specific codes (P0301-P0304). If present, start there
2. Inspect spark plugs (replace if >30k miles or visibly fouled)
3. Check ignition coils (swap suspect coil to another cylinder, see if code follows)
4. Check for vacuum leaks (causes lean misfire across all cylinders)
5. Check fuel pressure and injector spray pattern
6. Subaru: Check for head gasket leak (coolant loss, white smoke)
7. Advanced: Check valve clearances (EJ engines tend to tighten over time)
codes.notes: EJ25D/SOHC head gasket failure is EXTREMELY common. If coolant is low and misfire occurs after warmup, suspect head gaskets. Also common: spark plug tube seals leaking oil onto plugs.

common_causes: Low compression (valve, piston, or head gasket issue)
Vacuum leak near cylinder 1 intake runner
diagnostic_steps: 1. Swap ignition coil with another cylinder. If misfire moves, coil is bad
2. Swap spark plug with another cylinder. If misfire moves, plug is bad
3. Check spark plug condition (oil fouling, carbon buildup, electrode wear)
4. Test coil primary and secondary resistance (varies by model, check FSM)
5. Check fuel injector operation (use stethoscope or noid light)
6. Perform compression test on cylinder 1 (should be >120 psi, <10% variation)
7. If compression low: leak-down test to identify valves vs rings vs gasket
codes.notes: Subaru cylinder numbering: Cyl 1 = driver side front, Cyl 2 = passenger side front, Cyl 3 = driver side rear, Cyl 4 = passenger side rear. Oil fouling common on EJ25 due to valve cover gasket leaks into plug tubes.

codes.description: Fuel Tank Pressure Control Solenoid Valve (Subaru-specific)
common_causes: EVAP canister vent valve stuck closed
diagnostic_steps: 1. Check gas cap - tighten or replace if damaged
2. Inspect EVAP hoses for cracks or disconnection
3. Test purge solenoid (should click when energized)
4. Check fuel tank pressure sensor voltage
5. Perform EVAP smoke test to locate leaks
6. Clear code and drive 50+ miles to see if it returns
codes.notes: Often triggered by loose gas cap or topping off fuel tank. Not emissions-critical but will prevent passing inspection in some states.

diagnostic_steps: 1. Check sensor voltage (should be steady ~0.45V, ±0.1V variation)
2. Inspect sensor connector for corrosion or oil contamination
3. Check wiring harness for damage (common near exhaust heat)
4. Test sensor heater circuit (should have 12V on heater pins)
5. Inspect for exhaust leaks near sensor (affects readings)
6. Replace sensor if voltage erratic or stuck high/low
codes.notes: Sensor 2 = downstream (after catalyst). If paired with P0420, diagnose P0136 first - bad sensor can cause false P0420. Use OEM sensors when possible (aftermarket often unreliable).

codes.description: Idle Air Control System RPM Lower Than Expected
common_causes: Idle air control valve dirty or stuck
diagnostic_steps: 1. Clean throttle body and IAC valve (use throttle body cleaner)
2. Inspect for vacuum leaks (especially intake boots)
3. Check IAC valve operation (should move when commanded)
4. Perform idle relearn procedure (varies by year/model)
5. Check IAC valve resistance (typically 10-14 ohms)
6. Replace IAC if cleaning doesn't resolve issue
codes.notes: Subaru idle relearn: Disconnect battery for 10 min, reconnect, start engine and let idle for 10 min without touching throttle. Common after cleaning throttle body.
